coredata.dc:title: Porous Pt nanodendrites with exceptionally high oxidase-like mimic catalytic efficiency for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of ascorbic acid and acid phosphatase
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Yajun
Xie, Tiantian
Zhang, Jingyun
Liu, Wenyuan
Wang, Qiuxiang
Zhuang, Zhenjing
coredata.dc:description: In this study, three porous platinum nanodendrites (Pt NDs) with exceptionally high catalytic efficiencies were fabricated using a carbon-dot (CD)-mediated route. This study demonstrated that the as-prepared porous Pt NDs, such as the integration of a large number of small Pt nanozymes with excellent catalytic activity, exhibited larger sizes and higher oxidase-like catalytic efficiency. The highest Kcat value of the obtained porous Pt NDs was 8.61 × 104 s−1, which was 20-fold greater than that of HRP (Kcat = 4.3 × 103 s−1). A mechanistic study showed that superoxide radicals are likely key intermediates involved in oxidase-like catalysis. Furthermore, the porous Pt NDs exhibited excellent long-term and thermal stabilities and could be reused. Due to the exceptionally high oxide-like activity, the porous Pt NDs can catalyze the oxidation of ascorbic acid (AA) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD), forming two fluorescence emissions at 430 nm and 556 nm, respectively. A ratiometric fluorescence assay for AA and acid phosphatase (ACP) was established. The linear range for AA and ACP determination is 0.05–25 μmol·L−1 and 0.1–30 U·L−1, and the limit of detection was 0.036 μmol·L−1 and 0.03 U·L−1, respectively. Considering their high catalytic efficiency, this type of porous Pt NDs holds excellent potential for biosensor applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous
Nanodendrites
Pt
Nanozymes
Oxidase-like
Ratiometric fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Enzyme mimicking activities of the porous Pt NDs
Steady-state kinetics analysis of the porous Pt NDs
Synthesis and characterization of porous Pt NDs
Oxidase-like activity of porous Pt NDs
Catalytic efficiency of porous Pt NDs
Stability and reusable of porous Pt NDs
Ascorbic acid detection in the presence of porous Pt NDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Science and Technology of Quanzhou, Fujian Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the project of Science and Technology of Quanzhou, Fujian Province, China (No. 2023C021YR). The authors would like to express our sincere thanks to Instrumental Analysis Center of Huaqiao University for taking the TEM images, and XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of Pt NDs-1(A), Pt NDs-2 (B), and Pt NDs-3 (C). (D) XRD pattern of the obtained porous Pt NDs.

(A) UV − vis spectra of different reaction systems containing 0.1 mmol·L−1 TMB and 5 μg·mL−1 nanozymes. (B) O2-dependent catalytic oxidation of TMB by Pt NDs-1. Insets are corresponding photos in the air (left), O2-saturated (middle), and N2-saturated (right) reaction solution at room temperature. (C) Time-dependent absorbance at 652 nm measured from the reaction solutions containing 0.1 mmol·L−1 TMB and 5 μg·mL−1 nanozymes in PB buffer (20 mmol·L−1, pH 4.0) at room temperature.

Steady-state kinetic assay of the porous Pt NDs by varying concentration of TMB. Insert: the corresponding Lineweaver-Burk plot. (A) Pt NDs-1, (B) Pt NDs-2, (C) Pt NDs-3. (D) A histogram comparing the Kcat values of the different sizes of porous Pt NDs. Error bars in the plot indicate standard deviations of three independent measurements.
(A) Effect of different scavengers on the catalytic oxidation of TMB by Pt NDs-1. (B) ESR spectra of DMPO/O2 •− adducts obtained from DMPO + porous Pt NDs-1 DMSO solution.

(A) Fluorescence spectra in the presence of 1 mmol·L−1OPD, 10 μM AA, and 5 μg·mL−1 Pt NDs. (B) The fluorescence responses of the sensing system against AA concentration. (C) The linear relationships of ΔI430 nm/ΔI556 nm against AA concentrations. (D) Fluorescence responses toward different species. The error bars were obtained according to three independent experiments.

(A) The fluorescence responses of the sensing system against ACP concentration. (B) The linear relationships of ΔI430 nm/ΔI556 nm against ACP concentrations. The error bars were obtained according to three independent experiments.

head.ce:title: Porous Pt nanodendrites with exceptionally high oxidase-like mimic catalytic efficiency for ratiometric fluorescence sensing of ascorbic acid and acid phosphatase

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medicine, Huaqiao University, Quanzhou 362021, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medicine, Huaqiao University, Quanzhou 362021, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instrumental Analysis Center, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instrumental Analysis Center, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, three porous platinum nanodendrites (Pt NDs) with exceptionally high catalytic efficiencies were fabricated using a carbon-dot (CD)-mediated route. This study demonstrated that the as-prepared porous Pt NDs, such as the integration of a large number of small Pt nanozymes with excellent catalytic activity, exhibited larger sizes and higher oxidase-like catalytic efficiency. The highest Kcat value of the obtained porous Pt NDs was 8.61 × 104 s−1, which was 20-fold greater than that of HRP (Kcat = 4.3 × 103 s−1). A mechanistic study showed that superoxide radicals are likely key intermediates involved in oxidase-like catalysis. Furthermore, the porous Pt NDs exhibited excellent long-term and thermal stabilities and could be reused. Due to the exceptionally high oxide-like activity, the porous Pt NDs can catalyze the oxidation of ascorbic acid (AA) and o-phenylenediamine (OPD), forming two fluorescence emissions at 430 nm and 556 nm, respectively. A ratiometric fluorescence assay for AA and acid phosphatase (ACP) was established. The linear range for AA and ACP determination is 0.05–25 μmol·L−1 and 0.1–30 U·L−1, and the limit of detection was 0.036 μmol·L−1 and 0.03 U·L−1, respectively. Considering their high catalytic efficiency, this type of porous Pt NDs holds excellent potential for biosensor applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: There are a variety of substances involved in the metabolic process, such as various ions, bioactive small molecules, proteins, etc in living organisms. When the levels of these substances deviate from the normal range, they may cause many disease [1–4]. For example, malnutrition associated with ascorbic acid (AA) is related to cardiovascular disease, Alzheimer’s diasease and so on [1]. The variation of acid phosphatase (ACP) is extremely associated with several diseases, such as prostate cancer, multiple myeloma, and myelogenous leukemia [2]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop sensitive and specific assays for AA and ACP activities. Several strategies have been designed to monitor AA and ACP, including electrochemistry [5,6], spectrophotometry [7,8], and fluorescence [9,10]. The fluorescence method is notable due to its simplicity and sensitivity. Ratiometric fluorescence analysis techniques, which compare the relative intensities of two fluorescence emission peaks, effectively minimize systematic errors and enhance analytical accuracy [11,12]. Therefore, ratiometric fluorescence analysis systems for the detection of AA and ACP have attracted much interest [13–16].

Nanozymes with peroxidase or oxidase activity exhibit strong catalytic oxidation performance in the presence of hydrogen peroxide or dissolved oxygen, capable of catalyzing the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and AA. The oxidized OPD (oxOPD) emits fluorescence at 556 nm. Meanwhile, the oxidized AA interacts with OPD to form another fluorescent substance (3-(1,2-dihydroxyethyl)furo[3,4-b]quinoxaline-1(3H)-one), with a maximum emission wavelength of 430 nm. Thus, a ratiometric fluorescence sensing method (ΔI430 nm/ΔI556 nm) for measuring AA can be established based on their fluorescence responses at 430 and 556 nm [12–14]. Additionally, ACP catalyzes the hydrolysis of L-ascorbate 2-phosphate (AAP) into AA, hence, the method for determining AA can also be employed to assess the activity of ACP [17].

Unlike peroxidase-like nanozymes, oxidase-like nanozymes catalyze oxidation–reduction reactions using molecular oxygen without the need for hydrogen peroxide. This apparent advantage has brought increasing attention to oxidase-like nanozymes and their potential applications in the antibacterial, molecular sensing, and tumor therapy fields [18–20]. However, the catalytic efficiency of most oxidase-like nanozymes, as measured by the catalytic constant Kcat (where Kcat indicates the number of substrate molecules that a single nanozyme can convert per second), is relatively limited. This will have a significant impact on the performance of these nanozymes in their applications.

Pt is the most widely studied nanocatalyst and has been demonstrated to exhibit oxidase-like activity [21,22]. Porous Pt nanodendrites show excellent catalytic performance due to their high surface area with open pores and rich edge/corner atoms; thus, the synthesis of porous Pt nanodendrites (Pt NDs) is a highly effective strategy for improving their utilization and catalytic performance [23,24]. However, the surface chemistry of nanomaterials varies with the preparation method, which significantly affects their catalytic activity, particularly their enzyme-like activity [25,26]. The synthesis of porous Pt nanodendrites with superior oxidase-like activities and enhance catalytic efficiencies remains challenging.

In this study, a one-step efficient method was developed to fabricate porous Pt NDs mediated by carbon dots (CDs) via the reduction of H2PtCl6 by AA. Porous Pt NDs constructed from primary particles, such as those with nanocrystalline integration, exhibit excellent catalytic efficiency. After elucidating the catalytic mechanism, the porous Pt NDs with the highest oxidase-like activity were used to develop ratiometric fluorescence assays for AA and ACP. The proposed method showed excellent performance for AA and ACP detection, implied the potential application of Pt NDs in clinical testing.
The details about chemicals, reagents, and instrumentation are given in the Supporting Information.

Porous Pt NDs were synthesized by a CD-mediated method. Three different CDs were prepared according to the authors’ previous work and are shown in Supporting Information. To fabricate porous Pt NDs, the CDs solution was mixed with H2PtCl6 first, followed by the addition of AA. The mixture was shaken at 37℃. To improve the oxidase-like activity of the prepared porous Pt NDs, the experimental conditions were optimized, as shown in Fig. S1 and S2. The optimized experimental conditions are presented in Table S2. And then the samples were prepared under optimal conditions, collected by centrifugation and followed by ultrasonic washing with water. After 5 cycles of centrifugation and washing, the final samples were collected, dispersed in water, and stored at 4℃ for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzyme mimicking activities of the porous Pt NDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the oxidase activity, the obtained porous Pt NDs were dispersed in 20 mmol·L−1 phosphate buffer (PB) solution (pH 4.0) containing 3′,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and reacted at room temperature for 5 min. The change in the color of the solution was observed, and the absorption spectrum of the solution was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. To investigate the role of oxygen in this catalytic process, nitrogen was injected into the PB buffer containing TMB to remove oxygen from the solution or to obtain an oxygen-saturated solution by constantly feeding oxygen. Then, a fixed amount of porous Pt NDs was added, and changes in the corresponding UV–vis absorption spectra were monitored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state kinetics analysis of the porous Pt NDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the 20 mmol·L−1 PB buffer (pH 4.0) containing (2 μg·mL−1) porous Pt NDs, different concentrations of TMB were added, and the absorbance of the solution at 652 nm was measured over time to obtain the zero-order rate constant. The kinetic parameters Vmax and Km were calculated according to the Michaelis-Menten equation.

The catalytic constant Kcat was obtained from Kcat = Vmax /[E], where [E] is the molar concentration of the porous Pt NDs nanozyme, which was calculated using a simple cubic stacking model and 52 % space utilization (details are given in the Supporting Information).

Hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide anions (O2 •−), singlet oxygen (1O2), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and protons (h+) are common intermediates in nanocatalysts. Therefore, we added different radical scavengers to the process of nanozyme (Pt NDs-1)-catalyzed oxidation of TMB to investigate the possible intermediates. The scavengers for •OH, O2 •−, 1O2, H2O2, and h+ are respectively: isopropanol, benzoquinone, L-tryptophan, catalase, and ethanol. Additionally, Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as a scavenger for O2 •−, and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) was employed to further confirm the formation of O2 •−.

Typically, porous Pt NDs-1 (final concentration 5 μg·mL−1) were added to the PB buffer (20 mmol·L−1, pH 7.0) containing OPD (final concentration 1 mmol·L−1) and different concentrations of AA. After incubating the mixture at 37 °C for 10 min, the fluorescence spectrum of the mixtures was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer with excitation at 360 nm.

Possible interferents and compounds were tested to evaluate the specificity of the developed method. The concentrations of Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Glucose (Glu), d-fructose (D-Flu), lactose (Lac), sucrose (SUC), glutamic acid (LGA), L-histidine (L-His) was 100 μmol·L−1; L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), glycine (Gly), L-arginine (L-Arg), L-tryptophan (L-Try) was 50 μmol·L−1, nitrite, citrate and glutathione (GSH) was 10 μmol·L−1, CEA and PSA was 10 ng·mL−1, and HRP and CAT was 10 U·L−1, respectively. After adding various interferents to the sensing platform of Pt NDs-1 (5 μg·mL−1), OPD (1 mmol·L−1), and AA (10 μmol·L−1) in PB buffer (pH 7), and allowing the mixture to react at 37 °C for 10 min, the fluorescence spectra of each system were then measured with excitation at 360 nm.

To evaluate the activity of ACP, in PB buffer (pH 7) containing 0.2 mmol·L−1 AAP, various concentrations of ACP were added, and the mixture was allowed to react at 37 °C for 1 h. Afterward, Pt NDs-1 (5 μg·mL−1) and OPD (1 mmol·L−1) were added, and the reaction was continued for an additional 10 min. The fluorescence spectra of the final solution were measured when excitation at 360 nm. All annotated concentrations are final concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of porous Pt NDs
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies suggest that the formation of porous Pt NDs can be modulated by ligands with intermediate binding affinities for Pt ions [27–31]. The authors’ previous study showed that the strong binding affinity between nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and a Pt precursor favored the formation of porous nanodendrites [31]. However, the binding affinity of the ligand for the Pt ions affects the structure of the final material. CDs synthesized using different methods have different surface properties, affecting the performance and morphology of the products. Thus, three different CDs were prepared using various techniques to fabricate porous Pt NDs. To improve the oxidase-like catalytic activity, the synthesis conditions, including the concentration of CDs and reaction time, were optimized (Fig. S1 and S2) and are listed in Table S2. Three porous Pt NDs were obtained under the optimal experimental conditions and labeled Pt NDs-1, Pt NDs-2, and Pt NDs-3. The shapes and sizes of the obtained products were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. S3, the as-prepared nanoparticles were well-dispersed and had a uniform dendritic shape assembled from nanocrystals; however, their sizes varied depending on the CDs used, ranging from 45 to 65 nm. The TEM images (Fig. 1 A-C) clearly show that the dendritic nanoparticles were assembled from interconnected nanocrystals with sizes between 3 and 4 nm (Fig. S4), forming open porous cavities. Note that this type of porous and dendritic structure, which not only assembles a large number of small Pt nanoparticles into one but also provides abundant edge and corner atoms, is particularly favorable for improving catalytic performance, especially catalytic efficiency. The lattice distances of 0.195 and 0.227 nm (Fig. 1A-C I-III) correspond to the (200) and (1 1 1) planes of the Pt FCC structure, respectively [32]. The diffraction peaks in the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern at approximately 39.8°, 46.3°, 67.6°, and 81.4° are ascribed to the (1 1 1), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes of FCC Pt (Fig. 1D), respectively. The surface and elemental compositions of the obtained porous Pt NDs were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Fig. S5). The high-resolution Pt 4f spectra could be deconvoluted into two peaks originating from Pt(0) and Pt(2 + ), indicating that Pt was primarily in the form of Pt(0) and Pt(2 + ) and that Pt(0) was the predominant species in terms of peak intensity [30,31].

Based on the TEM, XRD, and XPS results, it can be concluded that porous Pt NDs were successfully prepared using the CD-mediated synthesis route. The three types of porous Pt NDs have similar morphologies and exposed crystal surfaces but are different in size. Thus, the formation mechanism of the porous Pt NDs was further investigated, as shown in the Supporting Information. It is speculated that the CDs serve as structure-directing agents in this system. The obtained porous Pt NDs, such as an integration composed of a large number of small Pt nanocrystals, are beneficial for improving the catalytic efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidase-like activity of porous Pt NDs
ce:section.ce:para: The oxidase-like activity of the obtained porous Pt NDs was investigated by the catalytic oxidation of 3′,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) in PB buffer (pH 4). For comparison, the catalytic activity of commercial Pt black was determined. As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the three types of porous Pt NDs prepared were all capable of effectively oxidizing TMB to form the blue product (oxTMB) with maximum absorbance at 652 nm (Fig. 2A). Furthermore, this catalytic reaction is oxygen-dependent. As shown in Fig. 2B, under the same experimental conditions, after removing dissolved oxygen from the reaction system, the color of the solution noticeably lightens, and the absorbance at 652 nm significantly decreases. Conversely, when oxygen is continuously introduced into the system, the blue color of the reaction solution noticeably deepens, and the absorbance at 652 nm significantly increases. These results confirm the intrinsic oxidase-like activity of the porous Pt NDs. Additionally, at the same mass concentration, the oxidation rate of TMB catalyzed by the three types of porous Pt NDs is all greater than that of commercial Pt black (Fig. 2C), indicating that these three types of porous Pt NDs possess superior catalytic activity and efficiency. This result demonstrates that the assembly of Pt nanocrystals into porous nanodendrites significantly improves molecular accessibility and enhances catalytic activity.

Considering that the reaction temperature and pH can affect the catalytic activity of the nanozyme, we used TMB as a chromogenic agent to optimize the catalytic conditions for the porous Pt NDs. As shown in Fig. S9, at a temperature of 40 °C and a medium pH of 4, the catalytic oxidation activity of porous Pt NDs is optimal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic efficiency of porous Pt NDs
ce:section.ce:para: Apparent steady-state kinetic assays were performed to quantify catalytic efficiencies. As shown in Fig. 3 , all sample-catalyzed TMB oxidation reactions obeyed Michaelis-Menten kinetics. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km ) and maximum reaction velocity (Vmax ) were calculated and listed in Table S3. Considering a nanoparticle as one copy of the enzyme for Kcat calculations, the catalytic efficiencies of the three types of porous Pt NDs in terms of Kcat values were also derived. As demonstrated in Table S3, all the porous Pt NDs exhibited very high Kcat values, which were far higher than those of the currently reported oxidase-like nanozymes [33], indicating highly high catalytic efficiency. Notably, among the three porous Pt NDs, the Kcat value of Pt NDs-1 (8.61 × 104 s−1) is the highest, 20 times greater than that of HRP (Kcat = 4.3 × 103 s−1) [34].

Generally, the smaller the particle size, the better the catalytic activity. However, in the present work, the Kcat values of the obtained porous Pt NDs increased with particle size and showed a good linear correlation (as shown in Fig. 3D), which may be caused by their structural particularity. As shown in Fig. 1, the prepared nanostructures consist of a large number of small nanocrystals connected. The sizes of the small nanocrystals that constitute the three types of Pt NDs were also statistically measured, which are 3.5 nm, 3.7 nm, and 3.7 nm, respectively (Fig. S4). Therefore, the Kcat values of these small nanocrystals can also be estimated (Table S4). From Table S4, the average Kcat value of the small Pt nanocrystals is 30.5 s−1 with an RSD of 6.07 %. The small RSD indicates the accuracy of the model we constructed in the process of calculating the Kcat values. So the larger the size of the Pt NDs, the more nanocrystals they contain, and finally, the larger the Kcat value. This study proposes that porous Pt NDs constructed using primary particles, such as by nanocrystals integration, show size-dependent catalytic efficiency.

To elucidate the mechanism of the oxidase-mimicking activity of porous Pt NDs, potential intermediates involved in the catalytic reaction were examined using a series of radical scavenging experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR). Pt NDs-1 was used as a representative for the radical scavenging experiments. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 A, the catalytic activity of porous Pt NDs-1 was evidently affected by benzoquinone, an O2 •− scavenger, suggesting that O2 •− radicals played an essential role in the porous Pt ND-catalyzed oxidation reaction [31]. Furthermore, we used the O2 •− specific fluorescence probe, hydroethidine (HE), to detect O2 •− radicals. As shown in Fig. S10, after adding HE to the porous Pt NDs-1 solution, a significant fluorescence emission at 610 nm was observed, and its intensity increased with the concentration of the porous Pt NDs-1. These results reveal the production of O2 •− intermediates. EPR was employed for the detection of O2 •− with 5,5′-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as the trapping agent. Fig. 4B presents the significant characteristic peak of DMPO/O2 •− adducts when Pt NDs-1 was mixed with DMPO, verifying the formation of O2 •− intermediates in the oxidase-like catalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and reusable of porous Pt NDs
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the potential applications of the obtained nanozymes, their stability was evaluated using Pt NDs-1 as a representative sample. As displayed in Fig. S11A, after six months of storage in water at 4℃, Pt NDs-1 showed no noticeable change in catalytic activity, suggesting that they had excellent long-term stability. The thermal stability was investigated by comparing the ability of Pt NDs-1 to catalyze TMB oxidation after half an hour of incubation at different temperatures. As shown in Fig. S11B, the change in absorbance of oxTMB at 652 nm was negligible below 50 °C, suggesting the good thermal stability of the porous Pt NDs. Furthermore, the RSD of ten successive catalytic oxidations of TMB by Pt NDs-1 adsorbed on the bottle wall was 1.58 % (Fig. S11C), indicating that the porous Pt ND nanozyme is stable and reusable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ascorbic acid detection in the presence of porous Pt NDs
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared nanozymes had superior oxide-like activity, which not only rapidly oxidized TMB but also oxidized a variety of chromogenic substrates and reductive substances, such as OPD and AA (Fig. S12). As shown in Fig. 5 A, the oxidation product of OPD exhibited a distinct fluorescence emission peak at 430 nm, and a new fluorescence peak appeared at 556 nm following the addition of AA, originating from the reaction between oxidized AA and OPD. Therefore, a fluorescence sensing method (ΔI430 nm/ΔI556 nm) for measuring AA was established based on the reverse fluorescence responses at 430 and 556 nm. The addition of AA not only depleted OPD but also shared competitive catalytic active sites on the nanozymes to inhibit oxOPD generation. Thus, the catalytic conditions were optimized to ensure the optimal dual-catalytic effect. As shown in Fig. S13, the effects of pH and temperature on the fluorescence intensities at 430 and 556 nm are reversed, and the pH = 7 and 37℃ were selected to achieve a satisfactory effect.

Under optimal conditions, with increasing AA concentration, the fluorescence intensity increased at 430 nm and decreased at 556 nm. As displayed in Fig. 5B and 5C, the ratio fluorescence intensity (ΔI430 nm/ΔI556 nm) and concentration of AA showed a good linear correlation in the concentration range of 0.05–25 μmol·L−1, with an R2 value of 0.995. The limit of detection (LOD) for AA was estimated to be 0.036 μmolˑL−1 (3 N/S), which demonstrated a good AA detection performance compared with those of many previous reports (Table S5). Possible interferents and compounds were tested to evaluate the specificity of the developed method. As shown in Fig. 5D, common coexisting species, such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Glucose, lactose, d-fructose, sucrose, glutamic acid, L-histidine, L-arginine, L-tryptophan, nitrite, glycine, citrate, L-phenylalanine, HRP, CEA, PSA and CAT did not have a noticeable influence on AA detection. The exact concentration of glutathione also had no significant effect on AA determination. However, when the GSH concentration increases further, N-ethylmaleimide should be added as a masking agent to reduce the interference in the AA assays. The above results indicate that the proposed method has a excellent specificity for AA detection.

Encouraged by the good detection performance of AA, the application of porous Pt NDs in ACP analysis was further explored, based on the fact that ACP catalyzes the hydrolysis of AAP to generate AA [14–17]. To improve the ACP analysis performance, the experimental conditions, including the hydrolysis time and temperature, were optimized (Fig. S14). As shown in Fig. 6 A, the fluorescence intensity ratio (ΔI430nm/ΔI556nm) increases with the increase of ACP concentration and exhibits a linear correlation with ACP concentration within the range of 0.1 to 30 U·L−1. The LOD for ACP was evaluated as 0.03 U·L−1 (3 N/S), which is superior to that obtained using most other methods (Table S6). This can be attributed to the high oxidase-like activity of porous Pt NDs-1. Subsequently, the method was then applied to detect the ACP activity in diluted human serum samples (1 %) from Huaqiao University Hospital. As listed in Table S7, the recoveries ranged from 91 % to 103 % with RSD values below 5 %, suggesting that the proposed ratiometric fluorescence assay for ACP detection is reliable and practical.

Porous Pt NDs were fabricated using a CD-mediated route under mild conditions. The proposed method is unique because of its simplicity and effectiveness. As-prepared porous Pt NDs, such as the integration of a large number of small nanoenzymes with excellent oxidase-like activity, exhibit exceptionally high oxidase-like catalytic efficiency with Kcat of 8.6 × 104 s−1. Porous Pt NDs were successfully used to construct a performance-ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for detecting AA and ACP. The excellent performance for AA and ACP detection suggests the potential application of Pt NDs in the development of clinical test kits.

Yajun Zhong: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tiantian Xie: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jingyun Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Wenyuan Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Qiuxiang Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Zhenjing Zhuang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the project of Science and Technology of Quanzhou, Fujian Province, China (No. 2023C021YR). The authors would like to express our sincere thanks to Instrumental Analysis Center of Huaqiao University for taking the TEM images, and XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.112277.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Convenient and highly sensitive electrochemical biosensor for monitoring acid phosphatase activity
